chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: In the second unit, we are going to discuss the geometric image formation process and basic camera models.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: We are going to see how points in 3D space are related to points on the 2D image plane.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: The principle of projection is an old one.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: As an example, we can take, for instance, the human eye where light is passing through a lens and then aperture

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: and is then collected by photo receptors on the retina.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: The same principle holds true for the first camera obscura, which is collecting light through a little tiny hole that's made into the wall of an otherwise entirely dark room.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: That gives rise to an image on the air wall and then all the way until photographic camera has come to life.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: Here is an example of the camera obscura where an entirely dark room is illuminated by the light that is passing through this tiny little hole and creating an image of the objects outside.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: The light of the scene outside is very dark room.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: The aperture is so small and there is a little light coming into the room.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: You have to adapt to the darkness of the room if you are standing inside the room to actually perceive something.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: It is actually just very little light. It is not very bright if you create such a camera obscura at home.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: This has been used. You can find this in various museums, by artists, here are some examples.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: On this website you find more examples where ordinary rooms have been illuminated using this principle of a very small pinhole and then an image has been captured inside that room with a very long shutter time and giving rise to installations like you can see here by this artist.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: The most basic physical pinhole camera can be described as a box that has a little hole inside one of the walls.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: The hole is collecting light or is letting a light pass through but it is letting light pass through only into the direction continuing where the light is coming from.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: We have a point here that the light passes through at all and because light travels linearly along the rays, that light is hitting this image plane, the sensor here on the back, exactly at one location.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: Because of this, the image that is projected is actually projected upside down onto that image plane behind this little hole in the wall which is called a focal point because all the light rays that pass through this hole must pass exactly through this hole, this focal point.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: However, when we consider camera models in this lecture, we typically consider the image plane to be in front of the focal point and not behind the focal point.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: So here is an example of, despite this being the physical model, the mathematical model looks like this here on the right.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: The distance from the focal point of this plane is the same as the distance of this image plane to this focal point but in this case the image plane is in front of the focal point.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: Now, in this case, the image of the 3D object here is of the same size and the same shape as the image if it is projected behind the focal point, the only difference is that it is mirrored.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And so both models are actually equivalent. They are just related by this mirroring.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: So they can be brought into each other using a proper change of image coordinates.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: Now, in this lecture, we are considering this mathematical camera model which has some advantages in the sense that the image is not projected upside down so we can directly see the image as we would see it as humans.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And that is also how digital cameras store images. They don't store the images upside down but they store them in the right orientation.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: So this is what we can use as a replacement for this model because both are equivalent up to very simple reflection.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: Now, if you talk about projection models, there are two basic projection models that are important. There are many more but these two are the ones that we are going to discuss here.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: If you want to know about our projection models, I recommend having a look into this. Let's keep up chapter 214.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: The first model that we are going to discuss here is this so called Autographic projection model where light is assumed to travel parallel to each other's ray.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And then projected in that parallel fashion onto that image plane.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And in the perspective projection model, which is the pinhole camera model, we have a focal point and all the light rays that pass from any 3D point to that focal point, all the light rays must pass through the focal point, are hitting the image plane at the intersection of that line with the image plane.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: As you can see that here in this case, the size of the object changes while here the size remains the same.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: The object has the same 3D size as the dimensions on the image plane.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And both of these projection models are relevant.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: The traditional projection model is really the perspective projection model that is implemented or that can approximate almost all the cameras that we know, like our smartphones, our digital cameras or DSLRs.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: But if we use a very large focal length, if we have a telephoto lens, then this perspective projection model can be well approximated by an Autographic projection model.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: The perspective affects vanish if we use a telephoto lens. And in some cases, that's actually wanted.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: For instance, in applications where we want to use optics for measurement, here on the left you can see a so-called telecentric lens that models exactly the Autographic projection process.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: Because if you model that process exactly, and you can measure distances, then afterwards, after projection on the image plane, you know that these distances correspond one to one to the distances in the real world.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: So this is used for inspection purposes, for instance.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: Here is a comparison between perspective and Autographic projection and what they do to the image that is formed and that there is a continuous transition in between these two.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And that's also what you're going to play with and experiment with in the exercise.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: So here on the left we have a classic, this is all the same cube, but here it's projected using a perspective projection where the vanishing point is in the vicinity and not at infinity.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And we can see that these edges of that 3d cube that are all parallel to each other in 3d are intersecting at this vanishing point that we see here on this image.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: Now, if we increase the focal length, we come to a weak perspective setting or closer to a weak perspective setting where this vanishing point moves outside this image domain that we're considering here.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And if we increase the focal length even further, if we move the camera infinitely far away from the object and at the same time increase the focal length to infinity,

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: which of course is physically impossible without such a telecentric lens, then we obtain an Autographic projection here where then all the lines that are parallel in 3d are actually also parallel in the image that is projected in the image that we have captured with the camera.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And here at the bottom you can see two examples of a perspective projection and a Autographic projection or close to Autographic projection of a real scene.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: So how can we describe these projections mathematically? Here we see the model for the Autographic projection.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And in all illustrations that I'm showing here, the illustrations are only showing the X and the C coordinate, the Y coordinate, you can think of as going into the image plane here of this camera coordinate system in red.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: But it's just difficult to draw in 3d and it's confusing so I draw everything just as this cut in X and C dimensions.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: So what we have here is the camera center, the origin where the camera coordinate system is defined is the X, Y, C coordinate system where the 3d coordinates of a 3d point in 3d space are defined.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And then in the Autographic projection model, the image coordinate system falls together with the X and the Y axis here only X illustrated again.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: So we have the X and the X axis of the image coordinate system in blue and the X axis of the camera coordinate system in red that are the same and the same holds true for the Y axis.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And now because the light rays travel parallel to the so called principle line here and parallel to each other, we know that if we have this point X, C stands for camera coordinates and S stands for screen coordinates or image coordinates.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: We know that the X and the Y coordinates are actually remaining the same.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: So everything that we have done in this projection is we have removed the C coordinate.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: But XS, so this is the 3d point and the non-bold version is the X coordinate of that 3d point.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And that X coordinate of the 3d point is projected to the same value in the image plane.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: So the X coordinate in screen coordinates is the same value as the X coordinate in the camera coordinates.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And the same holds true for the Y coordinate.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: So this is how another graphic projection of a 3d point, bold XC 3d space to pixel coordinates, bold XS in the 2d image domain here works.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: The Y and the X axis of the camera and the image coordinate systems are shared.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: The light rays are parallel to the C coordinate of the camera coordinate system.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And during the principal axis here in this black one, and during the projection, the C coordinate is dropped and X and Y remain the same.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And to highlight it again, the Y coordinate is not shown here for clarity, but you can think of the Y coordinate as going into the direction of the image plane.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: The direction we can see here, but the behavior is exactly the same.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: So how can we write this down in terms of linear algebra?

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: Well, we know that an autographic projection simply drops the C component of the 3d point, but leaves the other coordinates the same.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And so we can simply express this in inhomogeneous coordinates as such, where we have a 3d point multiplied with this 2 by 3 matrix where the last coordinate of this 3d point is multiplied with 0.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And the first and the second coordinate are copied.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And we have an equivalent representation in homogeneous coordinates here with these augmented vectors in camera coordinates in 3d space.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: So there's a 4d vector and here in screen coordinates, now this is a 3-dimensional vector.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: Where we take this 4-dimensional vector and the last element is a 1 because it's an augmented vector.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: So the last element of X, S, will also be a 1 in this case.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: But of course, again, this can be replaced by any homogeneous coordinates. It doesn't need to be augmented vectors.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: Autography, as I already mentioned, is exact for telecentric lenses and an approximation for telephoto lenses.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: Now, after projection, the distance of the 3d point from the image can't be recovered anymore, which is a property that holds true for whole projections, also for prospective projection, of course.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: So after the projection onto the image plane, the distance is unknown.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: Now, in practice, we're not going to use exactly that model that I've just shown because the image sensor typically measures in pixels.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: We define the image coordinate system in pixels, not in millimeters or meters.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And so we must scale the coordinates in order to be able to measure in pixels.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And that's called scaled photography. And so what we have here is simply replace these ones with an S scaling factor.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: So we can scale the X and Y coordinates that we're projecting and still dropping the C coordinate, of course.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And the unit of this scaling factor is pixel over meter or pixel over millimeter, depending on the unit of the 3d point in order to be able to convert the metric 3d points into pixels.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: So let's assume we have 10 pixels per millimeter and we have a point that is at X 10 millimeters, then we would convert this into 100 pixels.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And our photography structure and motion can be estimated simultaneously using close form factorization methods.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And we're going to see some of these methods in the next lecture.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: Now, having seen the simple autographic projection model, we come to the perspective projection model, which is a model that we need for almost all traditional cameras.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: Where light rays are focused at a single focal point, which is here the camera center.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: So here's an example of how a 3d point XC in camera coordinates projects to pixel coordinates XS in, so here's the image plane now in the screen coordinates.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: As you can see now, the image plane and image coordinate system are not collocated with the camera coordinate system anymore as before.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: But the image plane is displaced from out of the camera center and all the rays that are all the rays, all the light rays here must pass through this focal point through this camera center.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: So there's always the light ray that passes through the camera center, the pixel S XS and the 3d point XC. This is the constraint.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And the convention here is that the principal axis is, which is this one here, is autogonal to the image plane and the lines with the C axis.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: So it's called the principal axis.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And again, why the Y coordinate is not shown here for clarity, but it behaves similarly.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: So how does a 3d point now project mathematically to this point XS? Well, for this 3d point, we know the X coordinate and we know the C coordinate in 3d camera coordinates.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And for this image coordinate, we know the X coordinate on screen and pixels, let's say, and we know the focal length F.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: This is the focal length that is the distance of the image plane from the focal point.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: That's a parameter of cameras. If you have a zoom lens, we can change the focal length.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: That will change how big the object will appear on the image plane. You can already imagine changing this will change the size of the projection onto the image.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: Because the image plane moves, it translates left to right.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: Now, what is the relationship that we can infer from this geometric constellation?

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: We see that there's a set of equal triangles. So we have this triangle here, XS and F.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: So if we divide XS divided by F, we must have the same ratio as if we divide XC by CC, just by the principle of equal triangles.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: So we have this triangle, this big triangle here, and this little small triangle here.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And this is what I've written here on the right hand side.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: So in other words, I can solve this for XS by saying, well, the pixel coordinate X is equal to XC divided by CC multiplied with the focal length F.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And this is exactly the pinhole projection formula.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And it also holds for Y, of course. YS is equal to YC divided by CC times F.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: Now we can also formulate this mathematically in terms of a matrix multiplication if we use homogeneous coordinates.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: So as we've seen in perspective projection 3D points and camera coordinates are mapped to the image plane by dividing them by their C component and multiplying with the focal length.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: That's what we've just derived. And this is this expression here for both the X and the Y coordinates in regular in homogeneous coordinates.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: Now, while this is a nonlinear expression, because we have to divide by C in homogeneous coordinates, we can write this as a linear equation.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: So assume a matrix called a camera matrix or projection matrix.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: There's a free by four matrix here with the focal length on the diagonal and zeros in the last column.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And if we multiply this with the homogeneous four-dimensional augmented vector XC of the 3D point, then we obtain, well,

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: XCF, YCF, and then the third coordinate CC.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And if we now know this is a homogeneous three-dimensional homogeneous vector.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And if we normalize that homogeneous vector, inter-nogmented vector, where the last element is one, we obtain exactly F XC over CC and F YC over CC.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And the first two elements of this vector.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: By using this homogeneous representation, because we have these points that are all equivalent to each other,

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: and by the definition of conversion from homogeneous coordinates to in homogeneous coordinates, we are representing the projection process,

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: and that we can express this entire process here with a simple linear multiplication if we assume homogeneous coordinates.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And then afterwards do the conversion to in homogeneous coordinates by dividing by the third element of the homogeneous vector.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: So the important point here is this projection is linear when using homogeneous coordinates.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And after the projection again, as in the autographic case, it's impossible to recover the distance of the 3D point from the image.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: As little remark, the unit for F is typically chosen as pixels to convert metric 3D points into pixels.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: If you look at this expression here, if we have a 3D point that's expressed in meters, so we have X and C being defined as meters.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: But the screen coordinates are supposed to be in pixels.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And of course the unit of F has to be also defined in pixels, because the meters cancel here.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And so the only thing that remains is the screen coordinate in pixel.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And that's why the focal length in our models that we're using is often going to be defined in pixels.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: This is the most basic form of the pinhole projection principle, but we can add more parameters.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And one important parameter is the so-called principle point.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: Why do we need a principle point?

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: Well, in the previous model that I've explained, the image coordinate system, and this is now a 3D illustration of this one here,

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: the image coordinate system is defined on the principle axis, is centered on the principle axis, but it's inconvenient to store pixels with negative coordinates.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: Consider a pixel here that would have negative coordinates with respect to that coordinate system.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: So what is done is in practice is, and also I have already, as you can see here, oriented the coordinate system in a way that is convenient to store images.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: What we can do here is we can move that coordinate system to the top left of the image.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: So this is the viewpoint here, looking from behind, this is the top left corner of the image.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And so the first pixel here, well, if we move this coordinate system, we'll be located at 0 or 0, or 1, 1 if you use a 1-based coordinate system, which is much more convenient to store image arrays.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And that's what we typically do in practice.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: We don't consider negative pixels, but we consider it just positive pixels and defined image coordinate system here, which means that this principle point here must be added to the image coordinates, such that we obtain only positive coordinates.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And that's called the principle point CX and CY that's added to the pixel coordinates after this projection, there was this customer previous slide.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: So the complete perspective projection model without distortion is given by this equation here, where you can see that now, in addition to this multiplying the focal length,

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: with X divided by C, we have also now a translation by the principle point.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And we have done two more modifications here.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: So one modification is that we have allowed the focal length in X direction to differ from the focal length in Y direction.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And we have added a number scaling factor here that allows for, this is called this Q, that allows for modeling sensors that are not mounted perpendicular to the optical axis due to manufacturing inaccuracies.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: FX and FYI, however, would only be independent from each other if we have different pixel aspect ratios and sensors are manufactured typically pretty precise.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: So in practice, what we often do is we set FX equal to FY, and also this Q is typically very small, so that in practice often this Q is set to zero.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: But we have to model in almost all cases that we consider the principle point.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: Right, so while F can be set the same and S can be set to zero, the principle point is still an important parameter that we want to estimate the one model.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: Now, this is the full camera matrix that is defined through all of these parameters, and this is called the intrinsic matrix, because it's, and actually this is, well, this is the projection matrix,

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: the free by form matrix, but the free by free sub matrix here in front is called the calibration matrix K here in red, and this is called the intrinsic matrix, because it stores all the intrinsic parameters of the camera, everything that's intrinsic to the camera like the focal length, the sensor ratio, the other pixel ratio, the principle point, and so on.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And this is opposed to the so called extrinsic parameters, which is the camera pose with respect to some world coordinate system.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: That's why it's called intrinsic parameters.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And the projection then can be done in homogeneous coordinates by just taking this forward and mentioned homogeneous vector, this is representing a 3D point in camera coordinate system and projecting it by multiplying it with this matrix.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: Now, one thing that's nice about this homogeneous representation is that we can change transformations.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: So if a 3D point is not represented in camera coordinate systems, but is represented in the world coordinate system, we can change the transformation, the rigid body transformation that maps a point by multiplying it with this rotation and translation matrix.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: This is an Euclidean transformation that we've seen before.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: It multiplies that point, and afterwards multiplies that point that is now in camera coordinates with the intrinsic matrix.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: So we have the extrinsic and the intrinsic matrix here.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: On to the image plane, we can also do this by simply multiplying these two transformations together into one matrix.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And now we have just to do one matrix multiplication instead of two matrix multiplications.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And in many cases, that's very convenient and can also save time, be more efficient.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: So here's the mathematical formulation of this.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: So we have the screen coordinate, which is equal to the camera coordinate or camera, the 3D point in camera coordinates, multiplied with the intrinsic, which is equal to the point in world coordinate multiplied with the extrinsics and the intrinsic and then the matrix.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And then we can multiply these two matrices together.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: This is a free by three matrix and this is a free by four matrix.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: So obtain a free by four projection matrix that directly maps a point from world coordinates to the screen.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And this free by four projection matrix P can be pre computed, can be pre multiplied and then applied for all the points where we want to apply this projection on.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And sometimes it's preferable to use a full rank 4 by 4 projection matrix where instead of free by four matrix with the intrinsic and a 4 by 4 matrix for the extrinsics, we have also a 4 by 4 matrix for the intrinsic where we simply have added the last row of zeros and the one in the last element.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: Now this is of course if we multiply these together we get a 4 by 4 matrix.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And now we can take this homogeneous vector, this 4D vector representing a 3D point.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And project it through this matrix and obtain now a 4 dimensional homogeneous vector X tilde.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: But because we know this is a point that's on the image screen, we must still normalize this vector with respect to the third coordinate, not with respect to the fourth coordinate.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And so what we obtain is this expression here which is familiar because the first three coordinates are the same, third coordinate is one by definition.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And we have this projection here in the first two coordinates.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And then the last coordinate here is the inverse step as you can see here.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And so because this fourth component of this inhomogeneous 4D vector is the inverse step, if the inverse step is known, we can use this full rank matrix to also do the inverse.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: So assume we have this vector where we know the depth, we know the inverse step as well.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: Then we can also, now because of the full rank matrix, we can invert it and go from an image point to the 3D point.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And afterwards in order to extract the 3D point we need to normalize with respect to the fourth entry because now we have a point in 3D space.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: But of course we need to know the depth otherwise this wouldn't be possible.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: Good.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: Now the last aspect about the geometric image formation process I want to mention is the lens distortion.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: We haven't talked about lenses yet, but we'll talk about lenses in the next unit.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And lenses are important because as already mentioned, if we just use a very small pinhole, we get very little light on the sensor plane.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: So it's important to have a lens that collects light. But if we have a lens system, then often also there's a geometric distortion introduced.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And this linear projection model that we've seen before doesn't hold exactly anymore. There's linear lines in 3D that are projected onto curves in the image plane.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And this is called distortion.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: So the assumption of the linear projection that straight lines remain straight on the image plane is violated in practice due to this properties of the camera lenses.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And there's two types of distortions. There's radial distortion and tangential distortion.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And both of them luckily can be modeled for most camera models and most lens models relatively easily.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: So here's the formula that is most often used.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: What is done here is we take the point in camera coordinates and first do the normalization by the C coordinate.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: But we don't multiply the focal length yet.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And then we compute the radius. And then with these components x, y and r, we can multiply those with these.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And then we can multiply these polynomials to them that model the radial distortion and the tangential distortion.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: So we see here how the x, y point is transformed through this equation non-linearly to yield a point x prime.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: That is the distorted point. So from the undistorted point, we get a distorted point.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And then this distorted point is multiplied with the focal length. So this is now the focal length from the pinhole model and the principal point is added.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And so this is, this is everything that we need to do in order to also model very basic radial and tangential distortion.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And the nice thing about this is that while this looks ugly at first glance, the nice thing is that this can actually be undone.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: This effect is invertible because these transformations are typically monotonic.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: So we can pre-compute undistorted images from distorted images and then still apply the pinhole model directly on this undistorted images that have been simply pre-computed before using the image or the projection models that we want to use the simple pinhole projection model.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And that's what's typically used in practice. We are undistorting the images before using them.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: Now this is a very relatively simple model. More complex distortion models must be used for more complex lenses, like in particular white angle lenses that do not follow this simple polynomial model.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And here's some examples of how this looks like. You are probably familiar with this. There's an example of a checkerboard here.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And if we have so-called barrel distortion, we obtain the effect like here. If we have the inverse of that, the negative effect is called pin-cushion distortion and we get a projection that looks like this.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And you can see in both cases the lines are not projected to lines but to curves.

chunks.title: Lecture2.mp3
chunks.text: And also depending on the type of distortion, the projected image becomes smaller or larger. So you need to use a different crop on the image sensor to fully exploit the projected image.

text: In the second unit, we are going to discuss the geometric image formation process and basic camera models. We are going to see how points in 3D space are related to points on the 2D image plane. The principle of projection is an old one. As an example, we can take, for instance, the human eye where light is passing through a lens and then aperture and is then collected by photo receptors on the retina. The same principle holds true for the first camera obscura, which is collecting light through a little tiny hole that's made into the wall of an otherwise entirely dark room. That gives rise to an image on the air wall and then all the way until photographic camera has come to life. Here is an example of the camera obscura where an entirely dark room is illuminated by the light that is passing through this tiny little hole and creating an image of the objects outside. The light of the scene outside is very dark room. The aperture is so small and there is a little light coming into the room. You have to adapt to the darkness of the room if you are standing inside the room to actually perceive something. It is actually just very little light. It is not very bright if you create such a camera obscura at home. This has been used. You can find this in various museums, by artists, here are some examples. On this website you find more examples where ordinary rooms have been illuminated using this principle of a very small pinhole and then an image has been captured inside that room with a very long shutter time and giving rise to installations like you can see here by this artist. The most basic physical pinhole camera can be described as a box that has a little hole inside one of the walls. The hole is collecting light or is letting a light pass through but it is letting light pass through only into the direction continuing where the light is coming from. We have a point here that the light passes through at all and because light travels linearly along the rays, that light is hitting this image plane, the sensor here on the back, exactly at one location. Because of this, the image that is projected is actually projected upside down onto that image plane behind this little hole in the wall which is called a focal point because all the light rays that pass through this hole must pass exactly through this hole, this focal point. However, when we consider camera models in this lecture, we typically consider the image plane to be in front of the focal point and not behind the focal point. So here is an example of, despite this being the physical model, the mathematical model looks like this here on the right. The distance from the focal point of this plane is the same as the distance of this image plane to this focal point but in this case the image plane is in front of the focal point. Now, in this case, the image of the 3D object here is of the same size and the same shape as the image if it is projected behind the focal point, the only difference is that it is mirrored. And so both models are actually equivalent. They are just related by this mirroring. So they can be brought into each other using a proper change of image coordinates. Now, in this lecture, we are considering this mathematical camera model which has some advantages in the sense that the image is not projected upside down so we can directly see the image as we would see it as humans. And that is also how digital cameras store images. They don't store the images upside down but they store them in the right orientation. So this is what we can use as a replacement for this model because both are equivalent up to very simple reflection. Now, if you talk about projection models, there are two basic projection models that are important. There are many more but these two are the ones that we are going to discuss here. If you want to know about our projection models, I recommend having a look into this. Let's keep up chapter 214. The first model that we are going to discuss here is this so called Autographic projection model where light is assumed to travel parallel to each other's ray. And then projected in that parallel fashion onto that image plane. And in the perspective projection model, which is the pinhole camera model, we have a focal point and all the light rays that pass from any 3D point to that focal point, all the light rays must pass through the focal point, are hitting the image plane at the intersection of that line with the image plane. As you can see that here in this case, the size of the object changes while here the size remains the same. The object has the same 3D size as the dimensions on the image plane. And both of these projection models are relevant. The traditional projection model is really the perspective projection model that is implemented or that can approximate almost all the cameras that we know, like our smartphones, our digital cameras or DSLRs. But if we use a very large focal length, if we have a telephoto lens, then this perspective projection model can be well approximated by an Autographic projection model. The perspective affects vanish if we use a telephoto lens. And in some cases, that's actually wanted. For instance, in applications where we want to use optics for measurement, here on the left you can see a so-called telecentric lens that models exactly the Autographic projection process. Because if you model that process exactly, and you can measure distances, then afterwards, after projection on the image plane, you know that these distances correspond one to one to the distances in the real world. So this is used for inspection purposes, for instance. Here is a comparison between perspective and Autographic projection and what they do to the image that is formed and that there is a continuous transition in between these two. And that's also what you're going to play with and experiment with in the exercise. So here on the left we have a classic, this is all the same cube, but here it's projected using a perspective projection where the vanishing point is in the vicinity and not at infinity. And we can see that these edges of that 3d cube that are all parallel to each other in 3d are intersecting at this vanishing point that we see here on this image. Now, if we increase the focal length, we come to a weak perspective setting or closer to a weak perspective setting where this vanishing point moves outside this image domain that we're considering here. And if we increase the focal length even further, if we move the camera infinitely far away from the object and at the same time increase the focal length to infinity, which of course is physically impossible without such a telecentric lens, then we obtain an Autographic projection here where then all the lines that are parallel in 3d are actually also parallel in the image that is projected in the image that we have captured with the camera. And here at the bottom you can see two examples of a perspective projection and a Autographic projection or close to Autographic projection of a real scene. So how can we describe these projections mathematically? Here we see the model for the Autographic projection. And in all illustrations that I'm showing here, the illustrations are only showing the X and the C coordinate, the Y coordinate, you can think of as going into the image plane here of this camera coordinate system in red. But it's just difficult to draw in 3d and it's confusing so I draw everything just as this cut in X and C dimensions. So what we have here is the camera center, the origin where the camera coordinate system is defined is the X, Y, C coordinate system where the 3d coordinates of a 3d point in 3d space are defined. And then in the Autographic projection model, the image coordinate system falls together with the X and the Y axis here only X illustrated again. So we have the X and the X axis of the image coordinate system in blue and the X axis of the camera coordinate system in red that are the same and the same holds true for the Y axis. And now because the light rays travel parallel to the so called principle line here and parallel to each other, we know that if we have this point X, C stands for camera coordinates and S stands for screen coordinates or image coordinates. We know that the X and the Y coordinates are actually remaining the same. So everything that we have done in this projection is we have removed the C coordinate. But XS, so this is the 3d point and the non-bold version is the X coordinate of that 3d point. And that X coordinate of the 3d point is projected to the same value in the image plane. So the X coordinate in screen coordinates is the same value as the X coordinate in the camera coordinates. And the same holds true for the Y coordinate. So this is how another graphic projection of a 3d point, bold XC 3d space to pixel coordinates, bold XS in the 2d image domain here works. The Y and the X axis of the camera and the image coordinate systems are shared. The light rays are parallel to the C coordinate of the camera coordinate system. And during the principal axis here in this black one, and during the projection, the C coordinate is dropped and X and Y remain the same. And to highlight it again, the Y coordinate is not shown here for clarity, but you can think of the Y coordinate as going into the direction of the image plane. The direction we can see here, but the behavior is exactly the same. So how can we write this down in terms of linear algebra? Well, we know that an autographic projection simply drops the C component of the 3d point, but leaves the other coordinates the same. And so we can simply express this in inhomogeneous coordinates as such, where we have a 3d point multiplied with this 2 by 3 matrix where the last coordinate of this 3d point is multiplied with 0. And the first and the second coordinate are copied. And we have an equivalent representation in homogeneous coordinates here with these augmented vectors in camera coordinates in 3d space. So there's a 4d vector and here in screen coordinates, now this is a 3-dimensional vector. Where we take this 4-dimensional vector and the last element is a 1 because it's an augmented vector. So the last element of X, S, will also be a 1 in this case. But of course, again, this can be replaced by any homogeneous coordinates. It doesn't need to be augmented vectors. Autography, as I already mentioned, is exact for telecentric lenses and an approximation for telephoto lenses. Now, after projection, the distance of the 3d point from the image can't be recovered anymore, which is a property that holds true for whole projections, also for prospective projection, of course. So after the projection onto the image plane, the distance is unknown. Now, in practice, we're not going to use exactly that model that I've just shown because the image sensor typically measures in pixels. We define the image coordinate system in pixels, not in millimeters or meters. And so we must scale the coordinates in order to be able to measure in pixels. And that's called scaled photography. And so what we have here is simply replace these ones with an S scaling factor. So we can scale the X and Y coordinates that we're projecting and still dropping the C coordinate, of course. And the unit of this scaling factor is pixel over meter or pixel over millimeter, depending on the unit of the 3d point in order to be able to convert the metric 3d points into pixels. Where light rays are focused at a single focal point, which is here the camera center. As you can see now, the image plane and image coordinate system are not collocated with the camera coordinate system anymore as before. But the image plane is displaced from out of the camera center and all the rays that are all the rays, all the light rays here must pass through this focal point through this camera center. So there's always the light ray that passes through the camera center, the pixel S XS and the 3d point XC. This is the constraint. And the convention here is that the principal axis is, which is this one here, is autogonal to the image plane and the lines with the C axis. So it's called the principal axis. Well, for this 3d point, we know the X coordinate and we know the C coordinate in 3d camera coordinates. And for this image coordinate, we know the X coordinate on screen and pixels, let's say, and we know the focal length F. This is the focal length that is the distance of the image plane from the focal point. That's a parameter of cameras. If you have a zoom lens, we can change the focal length. Because the image plane moves, it translates left to right. Now, what is the relationship that we can infer from this geometric constellation? We see that there's a set of equal triangles. So we have this triangle here, XS and F. So if we divide XS divided by F, we must have the same ratio as if we divide XC by CC, just by the principle of equal triangles. So we have this triangle, this big triangle here, and this little small triangle here. And this is what I've written here on the right hand side. So in other words, I can solve this for XS by saying, well, the pixel coordinate X is equal to XC divided by CC multiplied with the focal length F. And this is exactly the pinhole projection formula. And it also holds for Y, of course. YS is equal to YC divided by CC times F. Now we can also formulate this mathematically in terms of a matrix multiplication if we use homogeneous coordinates. So as we've seen in perspective projection 3D points and camera coordinates are mapped to the image plane by dividing them by their C component and multiplying with the focal length. That's what we've just derived. And this is this expression here for both the X and the Y coordinates in regular in homogeneous coordinates. And if we multiply this with the homogeneous four-dimensional augmented vector XC of the 3D point, then we obtain, well, XCF, YCF, and then the third coordinate CC. And if we now know this is a homogeneous three-dimensional homogeneous vector. By using this homogeneous representation, because we have these points that are all equivalent to each other, and by the definition of conversion from homogeneous coordinates to in homogeneous coordinates, we are representing the projection process, and that we can express this entire process here with a simple linear multiplication if we assume homogeneous coordinates. And then afterwards do the conversion to in homogeneous coordinates by dividing by the third element of the homogeneous vector. So the important point here is this projection is linear when using homogeneous coordinates. And after the projection again, as in the autographic case, it's impossible to recover the distance of the 3D point from the image. And this is opposed to the so called extrinsic parameters, which is the camera pose with respect to some world coordinate system. That's why it's called intrinsic parameters.
